unit.title: The Civil War Study Tool
unit.subtitle: Get ready to ace your test! Learn vocabulary, practice questions, and track your progress.
unit.essentialQuestion: Is fighting necessary? If so, when and how is it effective?

Worlds of North & South: How did geography shape the economic, social, and political development of the North and the South?
Why did two regions of the same country come to feel like two different worlds by 1850?
What did the cotton gin change about the trajectory of slavery in America?
African Americans at Mid-Century: How did enslaved African Americans build family, community, faith, and culture under a system designed to deny them all four?
What does resistance look like when overt rebellion is met with deadly force?
How did the experience and culture of enslaved people shape American society beyond the institution of slavery itself?
A Dividing Nation: Why did political compromise stop working, and at what point did it become impossible?
How can a single court case like Dred Scott shift the trajectory of a nation?
Was the Civil War inevitable, and who or what is most responsible for it?
The Civil War: How did the war change in purpose between 1861 and 1865, and what made it change?
What roles did African Americans, women, and ordinary soldiers play in reshaping the war?
What does it mean for a war to end, and what was left unresolved at Appomattox?

typingPassages.title: Two Americas, One Country
typingPassages.source: Adapted for the Civil War unit
typingPassages.passage: By 1850, the United States looked like one country on a map but felt like two different ones in everyday life. The North and the South had grown so far apart that people began to think of themselves as belonging to a region first and a country second. This feeling has a name: sectionalism. Geography drove most of the difference. The North had cold winters, rocky soil, and fast moving rivers that could power machines, so Northerners built factories, mills, and cities. This was part of the Industrial Revolution, a worldwide shift from making goods by hand to making them in factories. The South had warm weather and rich soil that was perfect for growing cotton. Wealthy Southerners bought huge farms called plantations and forced enslaved people to work them. The Deep South cotton-growing region became known as the Cotton Kingdom. One invention made all of this possible. In 1793, Eli Whitney's cotton gin sped up the slow work of pulling seeds out of cotton. Cotton became a fortune crop, and slavery, which some had hoped would slowly fade away, instead grew much bigger. The two regions even fought over taxes. Northerners liked tariffs, taxes on imported goods, because tariffs protected their factories. Southerners hated tariffs because the South had no factories of its own to protect. By the middle of the century, the differences between the regions felt impossible to bridge.

typingPassages.title: Lives Built Under Slavery
typingPassages.source: Adapted for the Civil War unit
typingPassages.passage: By 1850, the United States had a population of about 23 million people. Of those, 3.6 million were African Americans, and the great majority of them lived in slavery. Slavery treated people as legal property. An enslaved person could be bought, sold, beaten, or separated from their family at the will of the person who claimed to own them. About three-quarters of enslaved people in the South worked as field hands, laboring from sunrise to sunset under the watchful eyes of overseers, who were paid to push them harder. A smaller number worked as house servants, doing the cooking, cleaning, and child care inside the slaveholder's home. The South used law to keep enslaved people under control. Strict slave codes limited where they could go, what they could own, and whether they could learn to read. After Nat Turner's Rebellion in 1831, those codes grew even harsher. But enslaved people resisted in every way they could. Some openly defied their owners. Most resisted quietly, by breaking tools, working slowly, or sneaking food. Thousands tried the riskiest resistance of all: running away. Escape was made possible by the Underground Railroad, a secret network of safe houses and brave volunteers. Harriet Tubman, who had escaped in 1849, returned to the South 13 times to lead others to freedom. Her followers nicknamed her Moses, and despite the bounties placed on her head, she was never caught.

typingPassages.title: When Compromise Stopped Working
typingPassages.source: Adapted for the Civil War unit
typingPassages.passage: For 40 years, the United States held itself together with compromises. Each compromise was a deal between the North and the South over the same hard question: would slavery spread into new western territories? In 1820, the Missouri Compromise admitted Missouri as a slave state and Maine as a free state, then drew a line across the Louisiana Purchase. North of the line, slavery was banned. South of it, slavery was allowed. The deal held until the United States won huge new territories from Mexico. In 1850, Henry Clay tried again. The Compromise of 1850 admitted California as a free state, ended the slave trade in Washington, D.C., and let New Mexico and Utah vote on slavery, an idea called popular sovereignty. To please the South, it also passed a tougher Fugitive Slave Law. That law made things worse. Northerners now had to help capture escaped enslaved people. Many refused. In 1852, Harriet Beecher Stowe published Uncle Tom's Cabin. The novel sold 300,000 copies in its first year and turned millions of Northerners against slavery. Two years later, the Kansas Nebraska Act let settlers vote on slavery and threw out the Missouri Compromise line. Settlers from both sides poured into Kansas, and the fighting that followed was so bloody that newspapers called the territory Bleeding Kansas. The 1857 Dred Scott decision shocked the North, and after Lincoln's election in 1860, eleven Southern states announced their secession from the Union.

typingPassages.title: Four Years That Remade America
typingPassages.source: Adapted for the Civil War unit
typingPassages.passage: When the Civil War began in April 1861, both sides expected a quick victory. The Union called for 75,000 volunteers. The Confederacy called for volunteers to defend Southern independence. The North had a huge advantage on paper. It had 22 million people compared to the South's 9 million. It had 90 percent of the country's factories and most of its railroads. The Confederacy had better military leaders, including Robert E. Lee, but almost no factories to make weapons. Both sides expected one big battle to settle everything. They got that battle in July 1861 at Bull Run, and the Union army was sent fleeing back to Washington. The war went on for four long years. In September 1862, the Battle of Antietam became the bloodiest single day in American history, and Lincoln used the Union's narrow victory to issue the Emancipation Proclamation, which transformed the war into a fight to end slavery. The summer of 1863 turned the war for good. Lee was defeated at the Battle of Gettysburg, and the Siege of Vicksburg gave the Union control of the Mississippi River. By 1864, Grant commanded all Union armies, and Sherman's total war through Georgia broke the South's ability to keep fighting. On April 9, 1865, Lee surrendered to Grant at Appomattox. About 750,000 soldiers had died. In December 1865, the 13th Amendment ended slavery throughout the entire country.

vocabulary.definition: Strong loyalty to one region of a country instead of to the country as a whole.
vocabulary.simpleExplanation: Imagine if you cared way more about your neighborhood than about your whole town. Now imagine that feeling getting so strong that you start to think your neighborhood and the next one over are basically two different places. That is what was happening between the North and South.
vocabulary.example: By the 1850s, sectionalism had grown so strong that Northern and Southern members of Congress could barely sit in the same room without arguing about slavery.
vocabulary.typingSnippet: Sectionalism is loyalty to one region instead of the whole country. In America before the Civil War, the North and South began to feel like two different countries. They had different economies, different ways of life, and very different views on slavery. This deep divide eventually pulled the nation apart.

vocabulary.definition: A system in which one person is treated as the legal property of another and forced to work without pay.
vocabulary.simpleExplanation: Slavery meant that one person could legally own another person, force them to work for free, and treat them like property instead of a human being. In America, this system was used to force Black people, mostly from Africa, to do back breaking work on Southern farms.
vocabulary.example: By 1860, about four million enslaved people lived in the United States, almost all of them in the South.
vocabulary.typingSnippet: Slavery in America forced millions of Black people to work without pay on Southern plantations. Enslaved people had no rights. They could be bought, sold, beaten, or separated from their families. The Southern economy depended on slavery, while the Northern economy did not. This difference became impossible to ignore.

vocabulary.definition: The movement to end slavery in the United States.
vocabulary.simpleExplanation: Abolitionism was the fight to get rid of slavery. People who joined this movement gave speeches, wrote books and newspapers, and helped enslaved people escape. Some risked their lives doing it. They believed slavery was simply wrong, no matter what the law said.
vocabulary.example: When Frederick Douglass spoke at abolitionist meetings, audiences were stunned to hear him describe slavery in his own words after escaping it himself.
vocabulary.typingSnippet: Abolitionism was the movement to end slavery. Abolitionists believed that no person should be allowed to own another. They printed newspapers, gave speeches, and helped people escape to freedom. Some, like Frederick Douglass, had been enslaved themselves. Their work slowly shifted public opinion in the North against slavery.

vocabulary.definition: An economic system based on large farms that used enslaved labor to grow cash crops like cotton, tobacco, and sugar.
vocabulary.simpleExplanation: The Southern economy was built on huge farms that grew one main crop, usually cotton, to sell. These farms only made money because the work was done by enslaved people who were never paid. Without slavery, the whole system would have fallen apart, which is why so many Southern leaders fought to keep it.
vocabulary.example: A single plantation in Mississippi might have hundreds of enslaved workers picking cotton from sunrise to sunset, earning the owner a fortune while the workers earned nothing.
vocabulary.typingSnippet: The plantation economy was the South's main way of making money. Large farms grew crops like cotton and tobacco to sell to the North and to Europe. The whole system depended on enslaved workers who were forced to do the labor for free. Plantation owners became some of the richest people in America.

vocabulary.definition: A formerly enslaved Black American who escaped to the North in 1838 and became one of the most powerful voices in the abolitionist movement.
vocabulary.simpleExplanation: Frederick Douglass was born into slavery and taught himself to read in secret. After escaping, he traveled the country telling his story so that white Americans could no longer pretend slavery was not so bad. His books and speeches were so good that some people refused to believe a Black man had written them.
vocabulary.example: In 1845, Frederick Douglass published his autobiography describing his life as an enslaved person, and it became a bestseller that turned thousands of readers against slavery.
vocabulary.typingSnippet: Frederick Douglass escaped slavery in 1838 and became the most famous Black abolitionist in America. He wrote a powerful autobiography about his life in slavery. He gave speeches across the North and even in Europe. He published his own newspaper, The North Star. His words helped change how Americans thought about slavery.

vocabulary.definition: A secret network of safe houses, hidden routes, and volunteers that helped enslaved people escape from the South to freedom.
vocabulary.simpleExplanation: Even though it had no trains and no tracks, this network used train words as code. A safe house was a station, the helpers were conductors, and the people escaping were passengers. Travelers moved at night and hid during the day. Getting caught meant being sent back into slavery, sometimes worse.
vocabulary.example: Harriet Tubman made about thirteen trips back into the South as an Underground Railroad conductor and helped around seventy enslaved people reach freedom.
vocabulary.typingSnippet: The Underground Railroad was a secret network that helped enslaved people escape to freedom. It had no real trains or tracks. Helpers, called conductors, guided travelers from one safe house to the next. Most escapes happened at night. The network reached from the South all the way to free states and Canada. Thousands made it to freedom this way.

vocabulary.definition: A violent 1831 uprising of enslaved people led by Nat Turner in Virginia, in which about sixty white people were killed before it was crushed.
vocabulary.simpleExplanation: Nat Turner believed God had told him to lead other enslaved people in a fight against their owners. For two days, his group attacked plantations in Virginia. The rebellion was stopped, and Turner was captured and hanged. After this, Southern states passed cruel new laws making it illegal to teach enslaved people to read, even though those laws made the underlying problem worse.
vocabulary.example: After Nat Turner's Rebellion, several Southern states made it illegal for enslaved people to learn to read or to gather in groups without a white person present.
vocabulary.typingSnippet: Nat Turner led the most famous slave rebellion in American history. In 1831, he and his followers attacked plantations in Virginia, killing about sixty white people. The rebellion was put down in two days, and Turner was hanged. Frightened Southern leaders responded with harsh new laws against enslaved people, but the rebellion showed that slavery could not last forever without a fight.

vocabulary.definition: A state in which slavery was illegal before the Civil War.
vocabulary.simpleExplanation: If you were in a free state, slavery was against the law. That did not mean Black Americans had equal rights there, because they often did not. But no one could legally own another person within the state's borders. Most free states were in the North.
vocabulary.example: When an enslaved person crossed from Kentucky into Ohio, a free state, they were no longer legally enslaved according to that state's law.
vocabulary.typingSnippet: A free state was one where slavery was illegal. Most free states were in the North, where the economy did not depend on enslaved labor. Free states still had racism and unfair laws against Black Americans. But within their borders, no one could legally own another person. The number of free states versus slave states was a constant political fight.

vocabulary.definition: A state in which slavery was legal before the Civil War.
vocabulary.simpleExplanation: In a slave state, owning another person was legal. Almost all slave states were in the South, where huge farms needed unpaid workers to function. As the country added new states, the question of whether each new one would be a slave state or a free state became one of the biggest fights in American politics.
vocabulary.example: When Missouri tried to enter the Union as a slave state in 1819, Northerners objected so fiercely that Congress had to write the Missouri Compromise.
vocabulary.typingSnippet: A slave state was one where slavery was legal. Slave states were in the South. Their economies depended on plantations worked by enslaved people. As America added new states, Northerners and Southerners argued bitterly about whether each one should be slave or free. Each new state shifted the balance of power in Congress, raising the stakes of every decision.

vocabulary.definition: A formerly enslaved Black American who escaped in 1849 and then risked her life repeatedly to lead others to freedom on the Underground Railroad.
vocabulary.simpleExplanation: Harriet Tubman could have stayed safely in the North after her own escape. Instead, she went back into the South about thirteen times to rescue other enslaved people, including her own family. Slave catchers offered huge rewards for her capture. She never got caught and never lost a single person on her trips.
vocabulary.example: Harriet Tubman was nicknamed "Moses" by the people she helped because, like the biblical figure, she led her people out of bondage.

vocabulary.definition: A white Northern newspaper editor who founded the anti slavery paper The Liberator in 1831 and demanded an immediate end to slavery.
vocabulary.simpleExplanation: Most early abolitionists wanted to end slavery slowly, over many years. William Lloyd Garrison said no, slavery must end now. His newspaper, The Liberator, attacked slavery in fierce language for thirty five years. Southerners hated him so much that one state offered a five thousand dollar reward for his arrest.
vocabulary.example: On the front page of his very first issue, William Lloyd Garrison wrote, "I will be heard," and he kept his word for the next three decades.

vocabulary.definition: The boundary line between Pennsylvania and Maryland that became the unofficial border between the free North and the slave South.
vocabulary.simpleExplanation: Two surveyors named Mason and Dixon drew this line in colonial times to settle a land argument. Nobody planned for it to mean anything bigger. But by the 1800s, Americans started using it as shorthand for the divide between the North and the South, between free states and slave states.
vocabulary.example: An enslaved person who crossed the Mason Dixon Line into Pennsylvania moved from a slave state into a free state.
vocabulary.typingSnippet: The Mason Dixon Line was an old surveyor's boundary that came to mean much more than a state border. By the 1800s, Americans used it to mark the divide between free states to the north and slave states to the south. Crossing it could mean the difference between freedom and bondage. Today the phrase still stands as shorthand for the line that split a nation.

vocabulary.definition: A machine invented by Eli Whitney in 1793 that quickly removed seeds from cotton fibers, making cotton far more profitable to grow.
vocabulary.simpleExplanation: Before this machine, cleaning seeds out of cotton by hand was so slow that growing cotton was barely worth it. The cotton gin sped up that step about fifty times. Suddenly cotton was a goldmine. Plantation owners planted more of it, which meant they wanted more enslaved workers, not fewer. A machine meant to save labor ended up trapping more people in slavery.
vocabulary.example: After the cotton gin was invented, the number of enslaved people in the South grew from about 700,000 in 1790 to nearly 4 million by 1860.
vocabulary.typingSnippet: The cotton gin was a machine invented by Eli Whitney in 1793 that pulled seeds out of cotton fibers. What once took a full day by hand could now be done in minutes. Cotton became wildly profitable, and plantations spread west across the South. Tragically, the cotton gin made slavery grow instead of fade away, because more cotton meant demand for more enslaved workers.

vocabulary.definition: An 1820 agreement that admitted Missouri as a slave state and Maine as a free state, and banned slavery in northern parts of the Louisiana Purchase.
vocabulary.simpleExplanation: By 1820, the country had eleven free states and eleven slave states. Adding Missouri would tip the balance. Congress made a deal: Missouri came in as a slave state, Maine came in as a free state, and a line was drawn across the country saying no more slavery north of it. People called it a compromise, but really it just postponed the fight.
vocabulary.example: Thomas Jefferson said the Missouri Compromise was like "a fire bell in the night," warning him that the slavery question would tear the country apart someday.
vocabulary.typingSnippet: The Missouri Compromise of 1820 tried to keep peace between free and slave states. Missouri became a slave state and Maine became a free state. Slavery was banned in the rest of the Louisiana Purchase north of a line at 36 degrees 30 minutes. The deal kept the balance, but it also drew a line that made the divide between North and South feel official.

vocabulary.definition: An 1846 proposal to ban slavery in any land taken from Mexico in the Mexican American War.
vocabulary.simpleExplanation: When the United States looked likely to win huge new lands from Mexico, a Northern congressman tried to make sure none of that land would have slavery. The South was furious. Even though the law failed, the fight over it showed how angry both sides were getting. From this moment on, every new piece of territory became a slavery argument.
vocabulary.example: After the Wilmot Proviso failed, Southern leaders started saying out loud, for the first time, that they might leave the Union if the North kept attacking slavery.
vocabulary.typingSnippet: The Wilmot Proviso was an 1846 proposal to ban slavery in any land taken from Mexico. It passed the House of Representatives but never made it through the Senate. Even though it failed, it forced Congress to argue openly about slavery in new western land. From this point on, the fight over slavery in the territories never went away.

vocabulary.definition: A package of five 1850 laws that admitted California as a free state and passed a tougher Fugitive Slave Law to please the South.
vocabulary.simpleExplanation: After the war with Mexico, the country had a huge new chunk of land and no agreement about slavery in it. Senator Henry Clay put together a deal with five parts. The North got California as a free state. The South got a much harsher law forcing Northerners to help return escaped enslaved people. Both sides walked away unhappy, which is how compromises sometimes work.
vocabulary.example: The Compromise of 1850 brought California into the Union as a free state, but the new Fugitive Slave Law it included turned even some moderate Northerners against the South.
vocabulary.typingSnippet: The Compromise of 1850 was a package of five laws meant to settle slavery fights after the Mexican American War. California became a free state. The slave trade was banned in Washington DC. A tougher Fugitive Slave Law was passed to please the South. The compromise delayed civil war by about ten years, but it left people on both sides angrier than before.

vocabulary.definition: A harsh 1850 law that required citizens in free states to help capture and return people who had escaped slavery.
vocabulary.simpleExplanation: Before this law, an enslaved person who reached a free state was usually safe. After it, slave catchers could chase people deep into the North, and ordinary Northerners could be punished for not helping. Suddenly slavery was no longer a Southern problem, it was happening in Northern towns and cities. Many Northerners who had been quiet about slavery now turned against it.
vocabulary.example: After the Fugitive Slave Law passed, abolitionists in cities like Boston physically blocked slave catchers from taking people back south, sometimes leading to riots.
vocabulary.typingSnippet: The Fugitive Slave Law of 1850 forced people in free states to help return enslaved people who had escaped. Anyone who refused could be fined or jailed. The law shocked Northerners who had ignored slavery before. Now slavery was happening in their own towns. Many Northerners who had not cared about slavery suddenly turned against it.

vocabulary.definition: An 1852 anti slavery novel by Harriet Beecher Stowe that became one of the bestselling books of the 1800s and turned many Northerners against slavery.
vocabulary.simpleExplanation: Most Northerners had never seen slavery up close. Then Harriet Beecher Stowe wrote a novel that took readers inside it. People sobbed over the characters. The book sold more than 300,000 copies in its first year, an enormous number back then. It changed how regular Northerners felt about slavery in a way that no political speech ever had.
vocabulary.example: When Harriet Beecher Stowe later met President Lincoln, he is said to have joked, "So you're the little woman who wrote the book that made this great war."
vocabulary.typingSnippet: Uncle Tom's Cabin was an 1852 anti slavery novel by Harriet Beecher Stowe. It told the story of an enslaved man named Tom and the cruelty he faced. The book sold hundreds of thousands of copies. Many Northerners cried while reading it and turned against slavery for the first time. Many Southerners called the book a lie and banned it from their states.

vocabulary.definition: A period of violent fighting in the Kansas Territory between 1854 and 1859 over whether Kansas would be a free state or a slave state.
vocabulary.simpleExplanation: When Congress let Kansas settlers vote on slavery, both sides rushed in to swing the result. Pro slavery Missourians crossed the border to vote illegally. Anti slavery families came in from the North. The two sides started attacking each other with guns, axes, and torches. People started calling the territory "Bleeding Kansas." It was a small civil war before the big one.
vocabulary.example: In Bleeding Kansas, an abolitionist named John Brown and his sons attacked and killed five pro slavery settlers, showing how brutal the fight had become.
vocabulary.typingSnippet: Bleeding Kansas was a period of violent fighting in the Kansas Territory from 1854 to 1859. Pro slavery and anti slavery settlers attacked each other with guns and torches. About fifty people died. The fighting started after Congress let Kansas voters decide whether the territory would be free or slave. It showed that compromise was no longer working.

vocabulary.definition: An 1857 Supreme Court ruling that said Black people were not citizens and that Congress could not ban slavery in the western territories.
vocabulary.simpleExplanation: Dred Scott was an enslaved man who sued for his freedom because his owner had taken him to live in free territory. The Supreme Court ruled against him in the worst possible way. The court said Black Americans had no rights at all and that Congress could not stop slavery from spreading. To Northerners, it felt like the South had taken over the federal government itself.
vocabulary.example: After the Dred Scott decision, even moderate Northerners worried that the next ruling could legalize slavery in their own free states.
vocabulary.typingSnippet: The Dred Scott decision of 1857 was a Supreme Court ruling that said Black Americans were not citizens and had no rights. It also said Congress could not ban slavery in the western territories. The decision was a huge victory for the South and a disaster for the North. Many Northerners now believed slavery would spread across the whole country if they did not stop it.

vocabulary.definition: Seven public debates in 1858 between Abraham Lincoln and Senator Stephen Douglas, with slavery as the main topic.
vocabulary.simpleExplanation: Two men running for one Senate seat in Illinois agreed to debate each other in front of huge crowds, and the country paid attention. Stephen Douglas argued that each state should decide on slavery for itself. Abraham Lincoln argued that slavery was a moral wrong that had to be stopped from spreading. Lincoln lost the Senate race but won something bigger: a national reputation.
vocabulary.example: Lincoln lost the 1858 Senate race to Douglas, but the debates made him so well known that he won the Republican nomination for president just two years later.
vocabulary.typingSnippet: The Lincoln Douglas Debates were seven public debates in 1858 between Abraham Lincoln and Senator Stephen Douglas of Illinois. They argued mostly about slavery. Lincoln said slavery was a moral wrong and should not spread. Douglas said each state should choose for itself. Lincoln lost the Senate election but became famous, which helped him win the presidency in 1860.

vocabulary.definition: An 1859 attempt by abolitionist John Brown to start a slave uprising by attacking the federal weapons storehouse at Harpers Ferry, Virginia.
vocabulary.simpleExplanation: John Brown was a white abolitionist who believed only violence could end slavery. He led 21 men in an attack on a federal armory in Virginia, planning to hand out the weapons to enslaved people and start a massive uprising. The plan failed quickly. Brown was hanged. But Southern leaders saw the raid as proof that the whole North wanted to come kill them in their beds.
vocabulary.example: After John Brown's Raid, Southern states began organizing their militias and stockpiling weapons, even before the 1860 election.
vocabulary.typingSnippet: John Brown's Raid happened in 1859 when abolitionist John Brown attacked the federal armory at Harpers Ferry, Virginia. He hoped to start a massive slave uprising. The raid failed in two days. Brown was captured and hanged. But the attack convinced many Southerners that the whole North wanted to attack them. The South began preparing for a war they now thought was coming.

vocabulary.definition: The presidential election won by Abraham Lincoln, an anti slavery Republican, without a single electoral vote from the South.
vocabulary.simpleExplanation: Lincoln did not call himself an abolitionist, but he had said clearly that slavery was wrong and should not spread. Southerners did not trust him. He won the presidency without being on the ballot in most of the South. Within six weeks of his election, South Carolina announced it was leaving the United States. Six more states followed before he was even sworn in.
vocabulary.example: Lincoln won the Election of 1860 with less than forty percent of the popular vote, but he won enough free states to take the Electoral College, and that was enough to terrify the South.
vocabulary.typingSnippet: The Election of 1860 was won by Abraham Lincoln, an anti slavery Republican. He did not appear on the ballot in most Southern states, and he won with less than forty percent of the national popular vote. But he won enough free states to take the Electoral College. Within weeks, South Carolina announced it was leaving the Union. The country was breaking apart.

vocabulary.definition: The formal withdrawal of a state from the United States.
vocabulary.simpleExplanation: Secession means leaving. After Lincoln won the 1860 election, Southern states announced one by one that they were no longer part of the United States. They formed their own country called the Confederate States of America. Lincoln said no state had the right to leave the Union. That disagreement, more than anything else, is why a war began.
vocabulary.example: South Carolina was the first to secede in December 1860, followed by Mississippi, Florida, Alabama, Georgia, Louisiana, and Texas before Lincoln was even inaugurated.
vocabulary.typingSnippet: Secession is the formal act of leaving a country. After Lincoln won the 1860 election, eleven Southern states seceded from the United States and formed the Confederate States of America. Lincoln said secession was illegal. The South said it was a state's right to leave. That disagreement could not be settled by talking. The Civil War began a few months later.

vocabulary.definition: An 1854 law that created the Kansas and Nebraska territories and let settlers vote on whether to allow slavery, throwing out the Missouri Compromise line.
vocabulary.typingSnippet: The Kansas Nebraska Act of 1854 let settlers in those territories vote on whether to allow slavery. It overturned the Missouri Compromise, which had banned slavery in that region for decades. Pro-slavery and anti-slavery settlers rushed in, and Kansas erupted into violence. The law meant to ease tensions, but instead it set the country on a faster path to civil war.
vocabulary.simpleExplanation: Senator Stephen Douglas wanted to build a railroad through the middle of the country, and he needed Southern votes to do it. So he wrote a law that let settlers in the new Kansas and Nebraska territories vote on slavery themselves. The Missouri Compromise had banned slavery there for 34 years. Douglas threw it out. Northerners were furious that their old deal had just been torn up, and angry settlers from both sides rushed into Kansas to fight over what came next.
vocabulary.example: The Kansas Nebraska Act of 1854 destroyed the political truce that had kept the country from breaking apart for decades.

vocabulary.definition: The idea that the people living in a territory should vote on whether to allow slavery there.
vocabulary.typingSnippet: Popular sovereignty was the idea that the people living in a territory should vote on whether to allow slavery there. Senator Stephen Douglas pushed this approach, hoping it would let Americans settle the question peacefully. In reality, popular sovereignty turned places like Kansas into battlegrounds. Letting voters decide sounded fair, but it could not solve a moral question with a ballot.
vocabulary.simpleExplanation: Politicians who supported popular sovereignty thought they had a clever solution: stop fighting in Washington and let the people in each new territory vote on slavery for themselves. It sounded fair and democratic. The problem is that you cannot vote on whether something is morally wrong. And in places like Kansas, the vote turned into a fistfight, then a gunfight, then a small war.
vocabulary.example: Stephen Douglas pushed popular sovereignty as a way to take the slavery question out of Congress, but it just moved the fight to Kansas instead.

vocabulary.definition: A powerful Democratic senator from Illinois who pushed popular sovereignty as a way to settle the slavery question.
vocabulary.simpleExplanation: Stephen Douglas was a short, loud, brilliant senator from Illinois who spent the 1850s trying to hold the Union together. His big idea was popular sovereignty: let new territories vote on slavery for themselves. He wrote the Kansas Nebraska Act. He debated Lincoln across Illinois in 1858 and won that race. But two years later, his Democratic Party split over slavery, which split the vote, and helped Lincoln win the White House. Douglas died in 1861, just weeks after the war began.
vocabulary.example: Stephen Douglas's Kansas Nebraska Act tried to settle the slavery question by letting voters decide, but it ended up triggering Bleeding Kansas instead.

vocabulary.definition: A federal fort in Charleston Harbor that Confederate forces fired on in April 1861, beginning the Civil War.
vocabulary.simpleExplanation: After South Carolina seceded, the federal government still held Fort Sumter, a small island fort in Charleston Harbor. Confederate leaders demanded the Union soldiers leave. Lincoln refused. After 34 hours of bombardment, the Union surrendered. No one was killed in combat, but the war had officially begun.
vocabulary.example: When Confederate cannons opened fire on Fort Sumter on April 12, 1861, the Civil War began without a single Union soldier dying in the battle itself.
vocabulary.typingSnippet: Fort Sumter was a federal fort in Charleston Harbor. After South Carolina seceded, Confederate leaders demanded that Union soldiers leave. Lincoln refused. On April 12, 1861, Confederate cannons opened fire. After 34 hours, the small Union garrison surrendered. The Civil War had begun. The attack united the North overnight.

vocabulary.definition: A September 1862 battle in Maryland that stopped Lee's first invasion of the North and remains the bloodiest single day in American history.
vocabulary.simpleExplanation: Lee invaded Maryland hoping a big Confederate victory on Northern soil would convince Britain and France to support the South. Instead, Union forces blocked him in twelve hours of brutal fighting. More Americans died on that single day than on any other in our history. The battle was technically a draw, but Lee retreated. Lincoln used the moment to change what the war was about.
vocabulary.example: After the Battle of Antietam, Lincoln issued the Emancipation Proclamation, transforming the war from a fight to save the Union into a war to end slavery.
vocabulary.typingSnippet: The Battle of Antietam was fought on September 17, 1862 in Maryland. It was the bloodiest single day in American history with over 22,000 casualties. Union forces stopped Lee's first invasion of the North. The Union victory gave Lincoln the chance to issue the Emancipation Proclamation. The war was no longer just about saving the Union. It was now also about ending slavery.

vocabulary.definition: A 47 day Union siege of the Confederate stronghold at Vicksburg, Mississippi, ending July 4, 1863, that gave the Union control of the Mississippi River.
vocabulary.simpleExplanation: Vicksburg sat on cliffs above the Mississippi River. Whoever controlled it controlled the river. General Grant surrounded the city for over six weeks until starvation forced the Confederates to surrender on July 4, 1863. The fall of Vicksburg cut the Confederacy in half. Texas, Arkansas, and Louisiana were now sealed off from the rest of the South.
vocabulary.example: After Vicksburg fell on July 4, 1863, residents of the city were so bitter about the date that they refused to celebrate Independence Day for over 80 years.
vocabulary.typingSnippet: The Siege of Vicksburg lasted 47 days. Union General Ulysses S. Grant surrounded the Confederate city in Mississippi until starvation forced its surrender on July 4, 1863. The Union now controlled the entire Mississippi River. The Confederacy was split in two. Vicksburg, combined with Gettysburg the day before, was a turning point of the war.

vocabulary.definition: A three day July 1863 battle in Pennsylvania that turned back Lee's second invasion of the North; the largest battle ever fought on American soil.
vocabulary.simpleExplanation: Lee invaded the North a second time, hoping a big victory might force the Union to negotiate. Instead, the two armies collided at the small town of Gettysburg, Pennsylvania. For three days they fought across rocky hills and open fields. On the last day, Lee ordered 12,000 men to charge the Union center. They were cut to pieces. Lee retreated, and the Confederacy never invaded the North again.
vocabulary.example: Four months after the Battle of Gettysburg, Lincoln delivered the Gettysburg Address at the dedication of the cemetery there, one of the most famous speeches in American history.
vocabulary.typingSnippet: The Battle of Gettysburg was fought July 1 to 3, 1863 in Pennsylvania. It was the largest battle ever on American soil with over 50,000 casualties. Union forces stopped Lee's second invasion of the North. On the last day, Lee ordered 12,000 men in Pickett's Charge across open fields. They were cut to pieces. Lee retreated. The Confederacy never invaded the North again.

vocabulary.definition: The Virginia courthouse where Confederate General Robert E. Lee surrendered to Union General Ulysses S. Grant on April 9, 1865, ending the Civil War.
vocabulary.simpleExplanation: After his army was trapped and starving, Lee met Grant in the parlor of a small Virginia farmhouse to surrender. Grant could have humiliated Lee. Instead, he let Confederate soldiers go home in peace and even shared his rations with the hungry Confederate troops. When Union soldiers started celebrating with cannon fire, Grant ordered them to stop. "The war is over," he said. "The rebels are our countrymen again."
vocabulary.example: Lee told his men, "There is nothing left for me to do but to go and see General Grant, and I would rather die a thousand deaths," before surrendering at Appomattox.
vocabulary.typingSnippet: Appomattox was the Virginia courthouse where General Lee surrendered to General Grant on April 9, 1865. The surrender ended the Civil War. Grant offered generous terms. Confederate soldiers could go home with their horses. Officers could keep their swords. Grant said the rebels were countrymen again. Five days later, Lincoln was assassinated.

vocabulary.definition: The 16th president of the United States, who led the Union through the Civil War and was assassinated five days after Lee's surrender.
vocabulary.simpleExplanation: Lincoln grew up poor in Kentucky and Indiana, taught himself law, and won the presidency in 1860 without a single Southern electoral vote. He never wavered from his belief that the Union had to be saved. He freed enslaved people in rebel states with the Emancipation Proclamation. He gave speeches that helped the country understand what it was fighting for. Five days after Lee surrendered, John Wilkes Booth shot him at Ford's Theatre.
vocabulary.example: Lincoln told the country at Gettysburg that the war was a test of whether "government of the people, by the people, for the people, shall not perish from the earth."
vocabulary.typingSnippet: Abraham Lincoln was the 16th president of the United States. He led the Union through the Civil War. He grew up poor and taught himself law. He won the 1860 election without a single Southern electoral vote. He preserved the Union, issued the Emancipation Proclamation, and gave the Gettysburg Address. He was assassinated five days after Lee surrendered at Appomattox.

vocabulary.definition: The president of the Confederate States of America for the entire Civil War.
vocabulary.simpleExplanation: Davis had served the United States as a senator and as Secretary of War before secession. When Mississippi left the Union, he reluctantly went with it and was chosen to lead the new Confederate government. He had military experience but little patience for politics, and he often clashed with Confederate state governors who refused to share resources. After the war, he was captured and held in prison for two years but never tried.
vocabulary.example: While Lincoln united the North during the war, Jefferson Davis struggled to keep Southern governors from hoarding their states' resources for themselves.
vocabulary.typingSnippet: Jefferson Davis was the president of the Confederate States of America for the entire Civil War. He was a former US senator from Mississippi and a former Secretary of War. He had military experience but little patience for politics. He clashed often with Southern governors. After the war, he was captured and imprisoned for two years but was never tried.

vocabulary.definition: The most famous Confederate general, who commanded the Army of Northern Virginia and surrendered to Grant at Appomattox in April 1865.
vocabulary.simpleExplanation: Lee had spent his whole career in the United States Army. When the war began, Lincoln offered him command of the Union forces. Lee refused, saying he could not raise his sword against Virginia. He became the Confederacy's most successful general, winning many battles against larger Union armies. After the war, he urged former Confederates to accept their defeat peacefully.
vocabulary.example: After Pickett's Charge failed at Gettysburg, Robert E. Lee rode out to meet his retreating men, telling them, "It is all my fault."
vocabulary.typingSnippet: Robert E. Lee was the most famous Confederate general. He commanded the Army of Northern Virginia. He had been a career officer in the US Army before the war. Lincoln offered him command of the Union forces, but he refused, saying he could not fight against Virginia. He won many battles against larger Union armies. He surrendered to Grant at Appomattox in 1865.

vocabulary.definition: The Union general who led the United States to victory in the Civil War, accepted Lee's surrender at Appomattox, and later became the 18th president.
vocabulary.simpleExplanation: Grant was the opposite of Lee in nearly every way. Lee was a polished aristocrat. Grant was a quiet, plain man who had failed at most things in life before the war. But he understood something other Union generals did not: that the war could only be won by relentless pressure, accepting heavy losses, and refusing to retreat. Lincoln finally found his fighter and put Grant in charge of everything in 1864.
vocabulary.example: When Lincoln was told that Grant drank too much, he reportedly replied, "Find out what brand of whiskey he uses, for I would like to send a barrel of it to each of my other generals."
vocabulary.typingSnippet: Ulysses S. Grant was the Union general who led the United States to victory in the Civil War. He won major battles at Shiloh, Vicksburg, and Chattanooga. Lincoln promoted him to command of all Union armies in 1864. He understood that the war required relentless pressure. He accepted Lee's surrender at Appomattox in 1865 and later became the 18th president.

vocabulary.definition: A Union general whose March to the Sea through Georgia in 1864 destroyed the South's ability to keep fighting; pioneer of total war.
vocabulary.simpleExplanation: Sherman believed war should be ugly so that people would never want it again. He marched his army through Georgia, burning Atlanta, tearing up railroads, and destroying farms and supplies along a 60 mile wide path of destruction. His army did not target civilians directly, but they made sure the South could no longer feed or supply its armies. The strategy was brutal and effective.
vocabulary.example: Sherman famously said, "War is hell," and he made sure the South believed him.
vocabulary.typingSnippet: William T. Sherman was a Union general who served under Grant. He led the campaign through Georgia and the Carolinas in 1864 and 1865. His March to the Sea destroyed the South's ability to keep fighting. He pioneered the strategy of total war, attacking not just armies but the resources that armies needed. His campaign helped Lincoln win re-election and helped end the war.

vocabulary.definition: An executive order issued by Lincoln on January 1, 1863, that declared all enslaved people in Confederate states to be free.
vocabulary.simpleExplanation: Lincoln waited for a Union victory to issue this order, because he did not want it to look like a desperate move. After Antietam, he announced it. The proclamation could not actually free anyone behind Confederate lines, since the Union army did not control that area yet. But it changed everything. Now any Union advance was a freedom march. And it kept Britain and France from supporting the South, since neither wanted to be seen defending slavery.
vocabulary.example: When Union armies marched into formerly Confederate territory after January 1, 1863, the Emancipation Proclamation meant that the enslaved people they encountered were legally free.
vocabulary.typingSnippet: The Emancipation Proclamation was an executive order issued by Lincoln on January 1, 1863. It declared all enslaved people in Confederate states to be free. It did not free people in border states loyal to the Union. The proclamation transformed the war into a fight to end slavery. It also kept Britain and France from supporting the South. Now any Union advance was also a freedom march.

vocabulary.definition: A short speech Lincoln gave on November 19, 1863, that redefined the war as a fight for human equality and democracy.
vocabulary.simpleExplanation: Lincoln was not even the main speaker that day. Another man, Edward Everett, gave a two hour speech first. Lincoln then spoke for two minutes. He said the war was about whether "government of the people, by the people, for the people" could survive. He said the dead had given "the last full measure of devotion." The speech was so short some newspapers thought it was a failure. Today it is one of the most famous speeches ever given.
vocabulary.example: Lincoln began the Gettysburg Address with the words "Four score and seven years ago," referring back 87 years to the Declaration of Independence and its claim that all men are created equal.
vocabulary.typingSnippet: The Gettysburg Address was a short speech given by Lincoln on November 19, 1863. He spoke at the dedication of the cemetery for Union soldiers killed at Gettysburg. The speech was just 272 words long and lasted about two minutes. Lincoln said the war was about whether government of the people could survive. It is now one of the most famous speeches in American history.

vocabulary.definition: A military strategy that attacks not just enemy soldiers but also the resources, supplies, and infrastructure that armies need to fight.
vocabulary.simpleExplanation: Most wars before this one had been fought between armies on a battlefield, with civilians left mostly alone. Total war breaks that rule. It says: if your factories make weapons, your factories are targets. If your railroads carry troops, your railroads are targets. If your farms feed soldiers, your farms are targets. It is a brutal strategy designed to make the enemy unable to keep fighting at all.
vocabulary.example: Sherman's army practiced total war by destroying $100 million in property across Georgia, including railroads, factories, and food supplies meant for Confederate troops.
vocabulary.typingSnippet: Total war is a military strategy that attacks not just enemy soldiers but also the resources, supplies, and infrastructure that armies need to fight. Sherman pioneered this strategy in his March to the Sea, destroying railroads, factories, and farms across Georgia. The goal was to make the South unable to keep fighting. The strategy was brutal but it worked.

vocabulary.definition: One of the first official Black regiments in the Union Army, famous for its 1863 assault on Fort Wagner in South Carolina.
vocabulary.simpleExplanation: When the war began, Black men were not allowed to fight in the Union Army. After the Emancipation Proclamation, that changed. The 54th Massachusetts was one of the first official Black regiments. Their attack on Fort Wagner in July 1863 was a tactical failure but a moral victory. They fought and died bravely while the country watched, and the country could no longer pretend Black men would not or could not fight for their own freedom.
vocabulary.example: The 1989 film Glory tells the story of the 54th Massachusetts and its assault on Fort Wagner, where the regiment lost about half its men.
vocabulary.typingSnippet: The 54th Massachusetts was one of the first official Black regiments in the Union Army. It was formed in 1863 after the Emancipation Proclamation allowed Black men to enlist. The regiment is best known for its assault on Fort Wagner in South Carolina, where about half its soldiers were killed or wounded. Their bravery helped open the door for nearly 180,000 Black soldiers to serve.

vocabulary.definition: An amendment to the United States Constitution, ratified in December 1865, that abolished slavery throughout the entire country.
vocabulary.simpleExplanation: The Emancipation Proclamation had only freed enslaved people in Confederate states. To make slavery illegal everywhere, you needed an amendment to the Constitution. The 13th Amendment did exactly that. It passed Congress in early 1865 and was ratified by the states in December of that year, eight months after Lincoln's death. For the first time in American history, no person could legally be owned by another anywhere in the United States.
vocabulary.example: When the 13th Amendment was ratified in December 1865, about four million formerly enslaved people became legally free across the entire United States, not just in former Confederate states.
vocabulary.typingSnippet: The 13th Amendment to the Constitution was ratified in December 1865. It abolished slavery throughout the entire United States, not just in rebel states. The Emancipation Proclamation had been a wartime order. The 13th Amendment was permanent law. About four million formerly enslaved people became legally free. But freedom on paper and freedom in practice were not the same thing.

vocabulary.definition: A famous Confederate general under Lee, who earned his nickname for standing firm under fire at the First Battle of Bull Run.
vocabulary.simpleExplanation: Thomas Jackson was a strict, deeply religious officer who became one of the Confederacy's most famous generals. At the First Battle of Bull Run, his men noticed he just stood there in the middle of cannon fire, calm as a stone wall. The nickname stuck. Lee relied on Jackson for his fastest, boldest moves. In 1863, his own men accidentally shot him at night during the Battle of Chancellorsville. He died eight days later, and Lee said he had lost his right arm.
vocabulary.example: Stonewall Jackson's death at Chancellorsville is often pointed to as the moment Confederate fortunes started to turn.

vocabulary.definition: A volunteer nurse who treated wounded soldiers on Civil War battlefields and later founded the American Red Cross.
vocabulary.simpleExplanation: When the Civil War began, military hospitals were chaos. Thousands of wounded men lay on battlefields with no real medical help. Clara Barton, a clerk in Washington with no medical training, started gathering supplies and going straight to the front lines herself. Soldiers called her the 'Angel of the Battlefield.' She bandaged wounds, wrote letters home for dying men, and helped track down soldiers who never came back. After the war she founded the American Red Cross, which still helps disaster victims today.
vocabulary.example: Clara Barton's work treating wounded soldiers under fire helped change how Americans thought about who could serve in war.

vocabulary.definition: A new type of Civil War bullet that expanded when fired and could hit a target accurately from 500 yards away.
vocabulary.simpleExplanation: Before the Civil War, an old musket could only really hit something accurately at about 50 yards. Then a French inventor came up with a new bullet shaped like a small egg with a hollow base. When it fired, the base expanded to grip the inside of the barrel, sending the bullet spinning straight and far. Suddenly soldiers could hit targets 500 yards away. Combined with old battlefield tactics where soldiers walked toward each other in lines, the Minie ball turned battles into slaughters.
vocabulary.example: The terrible casualty numbers at Antietam and Gettysburg were partly the result of generals using old tactics against new weapons like the Minie ball.

vocabulary.definition: United States Colored Troops, the official designation for regiments of Black soldiers in the Union Army.
vocabulary.simpleExplanation: USCT stands for United States Colored Troops, the official name for regiments of Black soldiers in the Union Army. After the Emancipation Proclamation, the Union began signing up Black volunteers, and almost 200,000 served by the war's end. They were paid less than white soldiers at first, and faced extra danger if captured. They fought in major battles, served as scouts, dug trenches, and earned 25 Medals of Honor. Their service made it impossible to argue, after the war, that Black men had not earned full citizenship.
vocabulary.example: By the end of the war, about one in every ten Union soldiers was a member of the USCT.

vocabulary.definition: Northern Democrats who opposed the Civil War and called for an immediate peace settlement with the Confederacy.
vocabulary.simpleExplanation: Not everyone in the North supported the war. Some Northern Democrats, especially in states like Ohio and Indiana, wanted Lincoln to negotiate peace with the Confederacy and let the South leave. Republicans called these antiwar Democrats Copperheads, after a poisonous snake. The Copperheads said the war was destroying the Constitution and the economy. They were never powerful enough to stop the war, but they did make Lincoln's reelection in 1864 a real fight.
vocabulary.example: Lincoln suspended habeas corpus partly to silence Copperhead newspapers that he believed were helping the Confederate cause.

vocabulary.definition: The Union's overall war strategy of blockading Southern ports, splitting the Confederacy at the Mississippi, and slowly squeezing the South into surrender.
vocabulary.simpleExplanation: Early in the war, an old general named Winfield Scott proposed a strategy that newspapers mocked as the 'Anaconda Plan,' after the snake that squeezes its prey to death. The plan had three parts: blockade Southern ports so no goods could come in or out, take control of the Mississippi River so the Confederacy was cut in half, and slowly press in from all sides. People laughed at first, but it turned out to be exactly how the Union won.
vocabulary.example: Grant's capture of Vicksburg in 1863 completed the Mississippi River part of the Anaconda Plan.

vocabulary.definition: The Deep South cotton-growing region whose economy and society depended on cotton grown by enslaved labor.
vocabulary.example: By 1860, cotton from the Cotton Kingdom made up about two-thirds of all American exports.
vocabulary.typingSnippet: The Cotton Kingdom was the broad belt of Southern states where cotton was king. From South Carolina across to Texas, plantations stretched for miles, worked by enslaved people. Cotton built fortunes, supplied northern factories, and tied the South to slavery more tightly each year. By 1860, this kingdom produced most of the world's cotton and shaped almost every Southern decision.
vocabulary.simpleExplanation: By the 1850s, a huge stretch of the Deep South was almost entirely about one crop: cotton. From South Carolina across to Texas, plantations grew cotton, traded cotton, and lived or died by the price of cotton. People started calling that whole region the Cotton Kingdom. Its wealth was real, but it depended completely on the unpaid labor of enslaved people.

vocabulary.definition: A major shift in the early 1800s from making goods by hand to making them in factories powered by water and steam.
vocabulary.example: The Industrial Revolution gave the North its mills, factories, and wage-paying jobs that drew immigrants to Northern cities.
vocabulary.typingSnippet: The Industrial Revolution was a wave of new machines, factories, and railroads that changed how people lived and worked. In the North, mills and factories drew workers into busy cities. Goods that once took weeks to make by hand could now be made in hours. The Industrial Revolution gave the North a very different economy from the farming South.
vocabulary.simpleExplanation: Up until about 1800, almost everything was made by hand at home or in small shops. The Industrial Revolution changed that. Machines powered by water and then steam started doing in minutes what used to take a person all day. Factories, mills, and railroads spread quickly. The North jumped on this change, while the South mostly stuck with farming. By 1860, the two regions did not even feel like the same country anymore.

vocabulary.definition: A small Southern farmer who owned and worked his own land, usually without enslaved laborers.
vocabulary.example: Yeoman farmers made up the majority of white Southerners, but the wealth and power of the South sat with the larger plantation owners.
vocabulary.typingSnippet: A yeoman farmer was a small landowner in the South who worked his own fields, usually without enslaved labor. Most white Southerners were yeoman farmers, not wealthy planters. They grew food for their families and a little extra to sell. Even though they did not own people, many supported slavery because it kept them above the bottom of Southern society.
vocabulary.simpleExplanation: When people picture the South before the war, they often imagine huge plantations. But most white Southerners were not plantation owners. They were yeoman farmers: regular families who owned a small piece of land, grew their own food, and worked the fields themselves. Most did not own enslaved people. Many still defended slavery, partly because the system kept them above the bottom of Southern society.

vocabulary.definition: A tax on imported goods, often used to protect domestic industries.
vocabulary.example: Northerners liked tariffs because they protected their factories, while Southerners hated them because they raised the price of goods they had to buy.
vocabulary.typingSnippet: A tariff is a tax on goods coming in from other countries. Northern factories liked tariffs because they made foreign products more expensive, helping American factories compete. Southern planters hated tariffs because they raised prices on goods they had to buy. Tariff fights showed how the two regions had opposite economic needs long before the war.
vocabulary.simpleExplanation: A tariff is a tax on goods coming in from other countries. It sounds boring, but tariffs were one of the biggest political fights of the 1800s. Northern factory owners loved high tariffs because they made foreign products more expensive, so people would buy American instead. Southern planters hated them, because the South made very little of its own goods and had to buy almost everything. Every tariff fight made the two regions angrier with each other.

vocabulary.definition: The shift of population from rural areas to cities.
vocabulary.example: Northern urbanization drew immigrants and former farmers into growing cities like New York, Boston, and Philadelphia.
vocabulary.typingSnippet: Urbanization is the movement of people from farms to cities. In the North, factories and ports pulled workers, immigrants, and young people into growing cities like New York, Philadelphia, and Boston. Cities meant new jobs, new ideas, and new problems like crowded housing. Urbanization made the North look and feel very different from the rural, agricultural South.
vocabulary.simpleExplanation: Urbanization just means people moving from the countryside into cities. In the early 1800s, this happened fast in the North. Factories needed workers. Immigrants poured into Boston, New York, and Philadelphia. Cities meant new jobs, new ideas, crowded apartments, and new problems. The South stayed mostly rural, so by 1860 a Northerner and a Southerner could live in completely different worlds without crossing the country.

vocabulary.definition: Strict Southern laws that controlled enslaved people's movement, work, gatherings, and ability to learn to read.
vocabulary.example: After Nat Turner's Rebellion in 1831, Southern states tightened their slave codes to make it illegal even to teach an enslaved person to read.
vocabulary.typingSnippet: Slave codes were laws in Southern states that controlled almost every part of an enslaved person's life. They banned reading and writing, travel without a pass, and gathering in groups. The codes also protected enslavers who used violence to keep control. By turning cruelty into law, slave codes tried to make resistance impossible, though they never fully succeeded.
vocabulary.simpleExplanation: Slave codes were Southern laws that controlled almost everything about an enslaved person's life. They could not travel without a pass. They could not gather in groups. They could not learn to read. They could not testify in court against a white person. The codes also protected white people who used violence to enforce slavery. The whole point was to make rebellion impossible by writing cruelty into the law itself.

vocabulary.definition: A person hired by a planter to supervise enslaved laborers and force them to work harder.
vocabulary.example: Overseers were paid to get the most cotton possible out of the field hands, and they often used whippings and threats to do it.
vocabulary.typingSnippet: An overseer was a hired man who managed enslaved workers in the fields on behalf of the plantation owner. He set the pace of work, punished anyone he thought slow, and reported back to the enslaver. Overseers were often paid based on how much cotton or sugar the plantation produced. That pay structure pushed many to rely on whips and threats to drive harder work.
vocabulary.simpleExplanation: Most large plantation owners did not actually run their own fields day to day. They hired an overseer to do it for them. The overseer's job was simple: get the most cotton out of the field hands as possible. He was usually paid based on the size of the harvest, which gave him every reason to push enslaved workers harder, often with whippings and threats. Many enslaved people remembered the overseer as the daily face of slavery's brutality.

vocabulary.definition: Religious songs created by enslaved African Americans, often containing hidden messages of hope, freedom, or escape.
vocabulary.example: The spiritual "Steal Away to Jesus" was sometimes a coded signal that a secret religious meeting was about to happen that night.
vocabulary.typingSnippet: Spirituals were religious songs created by enslaved African Americans that mixed African musical traditions with Christian stories. Songs like Go Down, Moses or Wade in the Water carried hope, sorrow, and sometimes hidden messages about escape. Singing together in fields or at night gave people strength and a sense of community. Spirituals are one of America's first true musical art forms.
vocabulary.simpleExplanation: Spirituals were religious songs that enslaved African Americans created out of two things they had: their faith and their pain. The melodies came from West African musical traditions. The words borrowed Bible stories, especially stories about freedom, like Moses leading his people out of slavery. Some spirituals were prayers. Others carried hidden messages about escape routes or secret meetings. Singing them together was one of the few moments enslaved people could feel fully like themselves.

vocabulary.definition: The hidden Black church that enslaved people created in secret, away from the white-controlled churches their masters made them attend.
vocabulary.example: The invisible institution met in clearings called "hush arbors," where Black preachers told the story of Moses leading his people out of slavery.
vocabulary.typingSnippet: The invisible institution was the secret religious life enslaved African Americans built away from white churches. They held services in cabins, fields, and forests, often at night. Their preaching, singing, and prayers blended African beliefs with Christianity. This hidden church gave enslaved people meaning, leadership, and a faith of their own that no enslaver could fully control.
vocabulary.simpleExplanation: Slaveholders made enslaved people attend white churches, where preachers gave sermons about obeying your master. So enslaved people built their own church in secret. They called themselves together at night in clearings and woods, where their own preachers told the parts of the Bible the white preachers left out. Historians later named this hidden church the invisible institution. It gave enslaved people something no master could control: a faith of their own.

vocabulary.definition: An enslaved person who worked in the fields growing crops like cotton, tobacco, or sugar.
vocabulary.example: About three-quarters of rural enslaved people were field hands, working from dawn until dark under the watch of an overseer.
vocabulary.typingSnippet: A field hand was an enslaved person who worked in the fields, usually planting, weeding, or picking cotton. Days were long, often from sunup to sundown, and the work was brutal. Children, women, and men all labored side by side under an overseer's watch. Field hands made up most of the enslaved population on Southern plantations.
vocabulary.simpleExplanation: About three out of every four enslaved people on Southern plantations worked as field hands. They planted, weeded, picked, and processed crops like cotton, sugar, or tobacco. The work was physically brutal and went from sunup to sundown, six days a week, in heat, rain, and cold. Children as young as eight or nine were sent to the fields. The wealth of the Cotton Kingdom was built directly on the bodies of field hands.

vocabulary.definition: An enslaved person who worked inside the slaveholder's house cooking, cleaning, or caring for children.
vocabulary.example: House servants worked closer to the family but lived under the constant watch of their master and could be sent to the fields as punishment.
vocabulary.simpleExplanation: House servants were enslaved people who worked inside the slaveholder's house instead of out in the fields. They cooked meals, cleaned, sewed, took care of the white children, and were on call almost every hour. From outside, this might have looked like easier work. In some ways it was. But house servants also lived under the constant watch of the master and mistress, with no privacy, and they could be sent back to the fields at any time as punishment.

vocabulary.definition: The family bonds enslaved people built and protected even though Southern law refused to recognize their marriages or parental rights.
vocabulary.example: Of all the things they endured, enslaved people most feared being sold away from their slave family.
vocabulary.typingSnippet: A slave family was an enslaved person's bond with parents, children, husbands, wives, and kin. The law did not protect these families, and enslavers could sell members away at any moment. Even so, enslaved people fought to stay connected through letters, stories, and chosen family. The slave family was a source of love and survival in a system designed to tear it apart.
vocabulary.simpleExplanation: Southern law said enslaved people could not legally marry or have legal parental rights over their own children. Owners could sell husbands, wives, and kids away from each other at any time. But enslaved people built families anyway. They held weddings their masters did not recognize. They gave their children secret last names. They told children about grandparents they would never get to meet. The slave family was an act of love and resistance in a system designed to destroy it.

vocabulary.definition: Cultural elements from West Africa that enslaved people preserved in America, including music, storytelling, food, and faith traditions.
vocabulary.example: African retentions can be heard in spirituals, seen in quilts, and tasted in foods like okra and gumbo that enslaved cooks brought into American kitchens.
vocabulary.typingSnippet: African retentions are pieces of African culture that enslaved people kept alive in America. They show up in foods like gumbo and okra, in rhythms and call-and-response singing, in funeral customs, and in folk tales. Even under brutal conditions, people held onto memory and identity. African retentions shaped American music, food, and language in ways the whole country still feels today.
vocabulary.simpleExplanation: African retentions are the pieces of African culture that enslaved people kept alive in America, even though slaveholders tried to strip everything African away from them. You can still hear retentions in the call-and-response of spirituals, taste them in foods like okra and gumbo, see them in quilt patterns and basket weaving, and feel them in funeral customs and dance. Enslaved people held onto memory and identity, and through them, the whole country picked up music, food, and language it would never have had otherwise.

vocabulary.definition: The United States as one nation, often used during the Civil War to mean the North and the federal government.
vocabulary.example: Lincoln's first goal in the war was not to end slavery but to preserve the Union by stopping the South from leaving it.
vocabulary.typingSnippet: The Union meant the United States as one nation under a single government. After Southern states seceded in 1861, Northerners called their side the Union and fought to keep the country whole. President Lincoln saw saving the Union as his most important job. By the war's end, the Union had survived, and the country would never again call itself a loose collection of separate states.
vocabulary.simpleExplanation: The Union meant the United States as one whole country instead of a collection of separate states going their own ways. After Southern states started leaving in 1861, Northerners called their side the Union, because that is what they were fighting for. President Lincoln said over and over that saving the Union was his most important job, even more important at first than ending slavery. By the end of the war, the country had been kept together, but only at a terrible cost.

vocabulary.definition: The Confederate States of America, the eleven Southern states that seceded from the Union and formed their own nation in 1861.
vocabulary.example: The Confederacy elected Jefferson Davis as its president and fought the Civil War to defend its independence and the institution of slavery.
vocabulary.simpleExplanation: After Lincoln's election in 1860, eleven Southern states left the United States and formed their own country: the Confederate States of America. They wrote a constitution that protected slavery, elected Jefferson Davis as their president, and set up a capital in Richmond, Virginia. The Confederacy lasted four years. It never won recognition from any major foreign country, and its surrender at Appomattox ended both the war and the experiment.

vocabulary.definition: The first major battle of the Civil War, fought in July 1861 in Virginia and won by the Confederacy.
vocabulary.example: After Bull Run, both sides realized the war would not be quick and began building large armies for a long fight.
vocabulary.typingSnippet: Bull Run, fought in July 1861, was the first big battle of the Civil War. Crowds from Washington even rode out to watch, expecting a quick Union victory. Instead, Confederate troops held firm, and General Stonewall Jackson earned his nickname there. The shocking Union defeat told both sides that this war would be long, bloody, and not won in a single afternoon.
vocabulary.simpleExplanation: Bull Run was the first big battle of the Civil War, fought in Virginia just three months after Fort Sumter. People in Washington were so sure the Union would win quickly that families packed picnic lunches and rode out to watch. Instead, the Confederates held firm, and a general named Thomas Jackson stood his ground so calmly that his men nicknamed him 'Stonewall.' The Union army broke and ran. Both sides realized the war was not going to end in a single afternoon.

vocabulary.definition: Unequal treatment based on a person's race, gender, religion, or other characteristic.
vocabulary.example: Even free Black Americans in the North faced discrimination in jobs, schools, and churches before the Civil War.
vocabulary.simpleExplanation: Discrimination is treating someone unfairly because of who they are: their race, their religion, their gender, where they come from. In the years before the Civil War, free Black Americans in the North faced discrimination almost everywhere. They were turned away from white schools, refused jobs, kept out of churches, and sometimes attacked in the street. Slavery was the worst form of discrimination, but it was not the only one.

vocabulary.definition: The belief that one race is superior to another, used to justify treating people unfairly.
vocabulary.example: Racism was the foundation of American slavery and continued to shape American life long after the war ended it.
vocabulary.simpleExplanation: Racism is the belief that one race is better or worse than another. It is not just personal prejudice. It is the thinking that gets used to justify treating whole groups of people unfairly. American slavery was built on racism: the idea that Black people were less than fully human and could be owned. Even after the war ended slavery, racism stayed deeply in American laws, schools, and neighborhoods for generations.

vocabulary.definition: The feeling of being weighed down or held back by severe and unfair force.
vocabulary.example: Spirituals gave voice to the oppression enslaved people lived under and to their hopes for freedom.
vocabulary.simpleExplanation: Oppression means being weighed down or held back by harsh, unfair power. It is the feeling of living under a force that wants to crush you. For enslaved people, oppression was every part of life: the law, the work, the violence, the rules about what they could read or where they could go. They survived through faith, family, and small acts of resistance, but the weight of the system never lifted while it lasted.

vocabulary.definition: The social separation of groups of people, especially by race.
vocabulary.example: Segregation kept Black children out of public schools in many Northern states even before the Civil War began.
vocabulary.simpleExplanation: Segregation is when people are kept separated by group, usually by race. Most Americans today think of segregation as a Southern thing, and yes, the South made it law after the Civil War. But it existed in the North long before the war too. Black children were kept out of white public schools. Black travelers were refused service at hotels and on stagecoaches. Segregation made sure that 'free' did not mean 'equal.'

vocabulary.definition: A person who flees or tries to escape, for example, an enslaved person escaping slavery.
vocabulary.example: The Fugitive Slave Act required Northerners to help return fugitive enslaved people to their owners in the South.
vocabulary.simpleExplanation: A fugitive is a person on the run, usually trying to escape something. In the years before the Civil War, the word usually meant a fugitive from slavery: a person who had escaped the South and was trying to reach safety in a free state or in Canada. Slave catchers chased fugitives across hundreds of miles. The Fugitive Slave Act of 1850 made every Northerner legally responsible for helping return fugitives, which is what turned thousands of quiet Northerners into outright abolitionists.

vocabulary.definition: The right of an arrested person to appear before a judge to determine whether their imprisonment is lawful.
vocabulary.example: During the Civil War, Lincoln suspended habeas corpus so the army could jail people suspected of disloyalty without a trial.
vocabulary.simpleExplanation: Habeas corpus is a legal protection that goes back hundreds of years. It means that if you are arrested, the government has to bring you in front of a judge and explain why you are being held. You cannot just be locked up and forgotten. During the Civil War, Lincoln suspended habeas corpus in some places so the army could arrest people suspected of helping the Confederacy without going through the courts. Some Americans said this saved the country. Others said it was exactly the kind of thing the Constitution was supposed to prevent.

practiceQuestions.question: What does the term sectionalism mean in the years before the Civil War?
options: Loyalty to a small section of one's town or city
Strong loyalty to one region of the country instead of to the country as a whole
The belief that the federal government should be divided into sections
Support for breaking the country into many smaller states
practiceQuestions.explanation: Sectionalism meant Northerners and Southerners thought of themselves as different peoples with different ways of life. That growing divide is what eventually pulled the country apart.

practiceQuestions.question: Why did the Northern and Southern economies become so different by the mid 1800s?
